title: C Intro
content:
C Introduction

The Mother of All Languages

C is a general-purpose procedural programming language created in 1972 by Dennis Ritchie at Bell Labs. It is incredibly fast, powerful, and forms the foundation of almost all modern computing, from operating systems (like Linux and Windows) to embedded systems and IoT devices.

  • Provides low-level access to computer memory.
  • Compiles directly to machine code for maximum performance.
  • The syntax of C heavily influenced C++, Java, JavaScript, and PHP!

title: C Syntax & Structure
content:
C Syntax and Structure

The main() Function

C programs are built using functions. Every C program MUST have a main() function—this is where the execution begins. Also, remember that every statement in C must end with a semicolon (;).

Basic C Program
#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
  // printf is used to output text to the screen
  printf("Hello World!\n");
  
  // Return 0 tells the OS the program ran successfully
  return 0;
}

title: C Output & Input
content:
C Output and Input

Talking to the User

To handle input and output, you must include the Standard Input/Output library (<stdio.h>). Use printf() to display output and scanf() to read input from the user.

I/O Example
#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
  int age;
  
  printf("Enter your age: ");
  // %d tells scanf to look for an integer.
  // &age tells scanf the exact memory address to store it.
  scanf("%d", &age);
  
  printf("You are %d years old.\n", age);
  return 0;
}

title: C Data Types
content:
Variables and Data Types

Strict Typing

C is a strongly typed language. You must declare a variable with a specific data type before you can use it. The type determines how much memory the variable takes up.

Primitive Types
#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
  int x = 5;               // Whole numbers
  float pi = 3.14f;        // Decimals (4 bytes)
  double precise = 9.999;  // Highly precise decimals (8 bytes)
  char letter = 'A';       // Single characters (must use single quotes)
  
  return 0;
}

title: C Operators
content:
C Operators

Math and Logic

Operators perform operations on variables and values. C provides a robust set of standard operators:

  • Arithmetic: +, -, *, /, % (modulo)
  • Assignment: =, +=, -=
  • Comparison: ==, !=, >, <=
  • Logical: && (AND), || (OR), ! (NOT)

title: C Conditionals
content:
C Conditionals

Decision Making

Use if, else if, else, and switch statements to execute different blocks of code based on specific conditions.

If/Else Example
#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
  int score = 85;
  
  if (score >= 90) {
    printf("Grade: A\n");
  } else if (score >= 80) {
    printf("Grade: B\n");
  } else {
    printf("Needs Improvement\n");
  }
  
  return 0;
}

title: C Loops
content:
C Loops

Repetition

Loops allow you to run a block of code multiple times. C provides the classic for, while, and do...while loops.

For Loop
#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
  // for (initialization; condition; increment)
  for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
    printf("Iteration %d\n", i);
  }
  return 0;
}

title: C Functions
content:
C Functions

Modular Code

Functions are reusable blocks of code. You define what data type the function will return (use void if it returns nothing), write the logic, and then "call" it from your `main()` function.

Custom Function
#include <stdio.h>

// A function that returns nothing (void)
void greet() {
  printf("Hello from a custom function!\n");
}

int main() {
  greet(); // Calling the function
  return 0;
}

title: C Arrays
content:
C Arrays

Contiguous Memory

Arrays store multiple values of the same type. In C, arrays are fixed in size and occupy contiguous (side-by-side) blocks of memory.



Array Indexing
#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
  // Declare an array of 5 integers
  int numbers[5] = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50};
  
  // Arrays are zero-indexed
  printf("The first number is: %d\n", numbers[0]);
  
  // Modifying an element
  numbers[1] = 99;
  printf("The second number is now: %d\n", numbers[1]);
  
  return 0;
}

title: C Strings
content:
C Strings

Character Arrays

Unlike Python or Java, C does not have a built-in "String" data type. Instead, a string is simply an array of char variables, ending with a special "null terminator" (\0) to tell the computer where the text ends.

String Syntax
#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
  // The compiler automatically adds the \0 at the end
  char msg[] = "Hello";
  
  // %s is the format specifier for strings
  printf("Message: %s\n", msg);
  
  return 0;
}

title: C Pointers
content:
C Pointers

Direct Memory Access

This is the most powerful (and dangerous) feature of C. A Pointer is a variable that stores the physical memory address of another variable, allowing you to manipulate memory directly.



Pointer Syntax
#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
  int x = 10;
  
  // Declare a pointer 'p' and store the address of 'x' using &
  int *p = &x;
  
  // Print the memory address (hexadecimal)
  printf("Memory address of x: %p\n", p);
  
  // Dereference the pointer using * to get the actual value
  printf("Value at that address: %d\n", *p);
  
  return 0;
}

title: C Structures
content:
C Structures

Custom Data Types

C doesn't have "Objects" or "Classes". Instead, it uses Structures (struct). A structure allows you to group different data types (like an int, a float, and a char array) together under a single name.



Struct Example
#include <stdio.h>
#include <string.h>

// Define the structure blueprint
struct Person {
  char name[30];
  int age;
};

int main() {
  struct Person p1;
  p1.age = 25;
  strcpy(p1.name, "Sanjith"); // Used to copy strings in C
  
  printf("%s is %d years old.\n", p1.name, p1.age);
  return 0;
}

title: C Memory Management
content:
Memory Management

Manual Control

Unlike Python or Java which automatically clean up unused memory (Garbage Collection), C makes you responsible. You allocate memory dynamically on the "Heap" using malloc() or calloc(), and you must manually return it to the system using free().

title: C File Handling
content:
File Handling in C

Persistent Data

C allows you to read from and write to files on your hard drive. You use a FILE* pointer to keep track of the file stream, and functions like fopen(), fprintf(), and fclose() to interact with it.

Writing to a File
#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
  // Open file in write mode ('w')
  FILE *fptr = fopen("data.txt", "w");
  
  if (fptr != NULL) {
    fprintf(fptr, "Writing this text to a file using C!\n");
    fclose(fptr); // Always close the stream!
  }
  
  return 0;
}

title: C Format Specifiers
content:
Format Specifiers

Formatting I/O

Because C is strongly typed, you must use special placeholders (Format Specifiers) to tell printf or scanf exactly what kind of data to expect.

  • %d – Integers (whole numbers)
  • %f – Floats (decimals)
  • %c – Single Character
  • %s – String (Character array)
  • %p – Memory Address (Pointer)

title: C Compilation
content:
Compilation and Execution

Building Machine Code

C is a compiled language. Unlike JavaScript which is interpreted on the fly by a browser, C source code must be translated entirely into raw 0s and 1s before it can run. You use a compiler like GCC to build an executable file.

title: C Best Practices
content:
C Best Practices

Writing Safe C Code

"With great power comes great responsibility." Because C gives you raw access to memory, it is easy to accidentally crash your program or create security vulnerabilities.

  • Free your memory: Always pair a malloc() with a free() to avoid memory leaks.
  • Initialize Pointers: Never leave a pointer uninitialized (a "wild pointer"). Set them to NULL if you don't have an address yet.
  • Beware Buffer Overflows: C will let you write 10 characters into a 5-character array. It will simply overwrite adjacent memory, leading to disastrous crashes.

title: C Summary
content:
C Summary 🚀

You Did It! 🏆

Congratulations! You have learned the foundations of C. By understanding C, you now understand how computer hardware actually interacts with software.

  • Basics: Variables, Format Specifiers, Conditionals, and Loops.
  • Architecture: Building modular code with Functions and Structs.
  • Under the Hood: Navigating memory using Arrays and Pointers.
  • System Operations: Compiling machine code and File I/O.

Knowing C makes you a drastically better programmer in higher-level languages like Java and Python. Keep coding!

title: C Pointers & Addresses
content:
C Pointers & Memory Address

Accessing Memory Directly

A pointer is a variable that stores the memory address of another variable. Use the reference operator (&) to get a variable’s physical address, and the dereference operator (*) to access or change the value at that address.



Pointer Syntax
#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
  int x = 10;
  int *p = &x; // p now holds the memory address of x
  
  printf("Value of x: %d\n", *p); // Dereferencing p gives 10
  return 0;
}

title: C Pointer Arithmetic
content:
Pointer Arithmetic

Moving Through Memory

Pointer arithmetic allows you to move a pointer to traverse array elements directly in memory. Adding 1 to a pointer doesn't just add the number 1; it moves the pointer forward by the exact byte size of its data type (e.g., 4 bytes for an int).



Array Traversal
#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
  int arr[4] = {10, 20, 30, 40};
  int *p = arr; // Arrays naturally act as pointers to their first element
  
  printf("Second element: %d\n", *(p + 1)); // Outputs 20
  
  p++; // Move pointer forward by one int
  printf("Pointer now points to: %d\n", *p); // Outputs 20
  
  return 0;
}

title: C Dynamic Memory
content:
Dynamic Memory Allocation

The Heap

C lets you allocate memory at runtime using malloc(), calloc(), and realloc(). This is incredibly useful when you don’t know how much memory you’ll need ahead of time (like a resizable array). You must ALWAYS free this memory with free() to prevent memory leaks!



malloc() and free()
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h> // Required for memory functions

int main() {
  int *ptr;
  // Allocate exact memory for 5 integers
  ptr = malloc(5 * sizeof(int));
  
  if (ptr == NULL) {
    printf("Memory allocation failed!\n");
    return 1;
  }
  
  free(ptr); // Release memory back to the OS
  return 0;
}

title: C Accessing Dynamic Mem
content:
Accessing Dynamic Memory

Using Pointers as Arrays

Once you allocate memory dynamically, you can use the pointer exactly like an array to read and write data to that specific block of memory.

calloc() Example
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

int main() {
  // calloc allocates memory AND initializes it all to zero
  int *p = calloc(4, sizeof(int));
  
  p[0] = 5;
  p[1] = 15;
  
  printf("Value at index 1: %d\n", p[1]);
  
  free(p); // Always clean up!
  return 0;
}

title: C Struct Pointers
content:
Pointers with Structs

The Arrow Operator (->)

You can create a struct dynamically using malloc(). When accessing the members of a struct via a pointer, you must use the arrow operator (->) instead of the standard dot (.) operator.



Dynamic Structs
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>

struct Car { 
  char brand[50]; 
  int year; 
};

int main() {
  struct Car *c = malloc(sizeof(struct Car));
  
  // Use -> to access struct members through a pointer
  strcpy(c->brand, "Honda");
  c->year = 2024;
  
  printf("Car: %s (%d)\n", c->brand, c->year);
  
  free(c);
  return 0;
}

title: C Double Pointers
content:
Pointer to Pointer

Multiple Indirection

A pointer can store the address of another pointer. This is known as multiple indirection or a "double pointer" (**). This is often used in advanced memory management, like dynamically allocating 2D arrays.



Double Pointer Example
#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
  int x = 5;
  int *p = &x;       // p holds address of x
  int **pp = &p;     // pp holds address of pointer p
  
  // Dereference twice to get the original value!
  printf("Value of x via pp: %d\n", **pp);
  
  return 0;
}

title: C Enums
content:
C Enums

Named Constants

Enums (Enumerations) define a custom data type that consists of a set of named integer constants. They are incredibly useful for improving code readability (e.g., using words like Mon instead of the number 0).

Enum Syntax
#include <stdio.h>

// By default, Mon=0, Tue=1, Wed=2...
enum Week {Mon, Tue, Wed};

int main() {
  enum Week today = Tue;
  
  printf("Tuesday is day number: %d\n", today);
  return 0;
}

title: C File Handling
content:
File Handling

Persistent Storage

C lets you create, open, read, write, and close files using FILE* pointers and functions like fopen(), fread(), fprintf(), and fclose().

Writing Files
#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
  FILE *f = fopen("data.txt", "w");
  
  if(f != NULL) {
    fprintf(f, "Hello World!\n");
    fclose(f); // Save and close
    printf("File written successfully.\n");
  }
  
  return 0;
}

title: C Memory Example
content:
Memory Management Example

Putting it Together

Real-world applications heavily rely on dynamic memory to create resizable lists (like a Python List or Java ArrayList). Using malloc to create the initial block, and realloc to expand the block as more data is added, avoids memory waste and allows dynamic expansion during runtime.

title: C Advanced Functions
content:
Advanced Function Concepts

Recursion and Callbacks

Functions in C are powerful tools. Recursion is when a function calls itself to break down complex problems (like sorting algorithms). Function Pointers allow you to store the memory address of a function, enabling you to pass functions as arguments to other functions (Callbacks) for highly flexible code design.

title: C Bitwise Operators
content:
C Bitwise Operators

Flipping Bits

Bitwise operators perform manipulations directly on the individual binary bits (0s and 1s) of a number. They are heavily used in low-level programming, cryptography, and embedded systems to save extreme amounts of memory and CPU cycles.



  • & (Bitwise AND)
  • | (Bitwise OR)
  • ^ (Bitwise XOR)
  • ~ (Bitwise NOT / Invert)
  • << (Left Shift) and >> (Right Shift)

title: C Advanced Summary
content:
C Complete Course Summary 🚀

You Did It! 🏆

Congratulations! You have completed the Advanced C curriculum. You now have the skills required to write true systems-level software.

  • Memory Mastery: Pointers, Double Pointers, and Pointer Arithmetic.
  • Dynamic Control: Managing the Heap with malloc, calloc, realloc, and free.
  • Advanced Architecture: Struct pointers, Enums, and File I/O.
  • Low-Level Logic: Manipulating binary data with Bitwise Operators.

These are the essential building blocks for writing real-world C applications, building operating systems, and programming hardware directly!

title: C Pointers & Arrays
content:
Pointers and Arrays

The Secret Connection

In C, arrays and pointers are intimately linked. The name of an array is actually just a pointer to its first element! Because of this, you can access array elements using pointer arithmetic instead of traditional indexing.



Pointer Iteration
#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
  int arr[4] = {25, 50, 75, 100};
  int *ptr = arr; // ptr now points to arr[0]
  
  for (int i = 0; i < 4; i++) {
    // *(ptr + i) is exactly the same as arr[i]!
    printf("Element %d: %d\n", i, *(ptr + i));
  }
  return 0;
}

title: C Header Files
content:
C Header Files

Organizing Code

Header files (ending in .h) contain function declarations and macro definitions that you can share across multiple C programs. They keep your code organized and prevent you from having to rewrite the same functions in every file.

Including Headers
// Use angle brackets <> for standard system libraries
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

// Use double quotes "" for your own custom local header files
#include "my_custom_header.h"

int main() {
  printf("Headers loaded successfully!\n");
  return 0;
}

title: C Preprocessor
content:
C Preprocessor Directives

Before Compilation

The C preprocessor is a macro processor that transforms your program before the actual compiler even sees it. It handles directives (commands that start with #) to include files, define constants, and perform conditional compilation.



Directives Example
#include <stdio.h>
#define PI 3.14159
#define DEBUG

int main() {
  printf("Value of PI is: %f\n", PI);
  
// This code will ONLY compile if DEBUG is defined above!
#ifdef DEBUG
  printf("--- Debug mode is ACTIVE ---\n");
#endif
  
  return 0;
}

title: C Macros
content:
Macros in C

Text Substitution

Macros are defined using #define. They act like functions, but they are faster because the preprocessor simply copies and pastes the text directly into your code before it compiles, completely avoiding the overhead of a real function call.

Macro Functions
#include <stdio.h>

// A macro that takes arguments to find the maximum value
#define MAX(a, b) ((a) > (b) ? (a) : (b))

int main() {
  int val = MAX(5, 10);
  
  printf("The maximum value is: %d\n", val);
  return 0;
}

title: C Storage Classes
content:
Storage Classes in C

Variable Lifetimes

Storage classes determine the scope, lifetime, and visibility of variables in C.



  • auto: The default for all local variables. Destroyed when the function ends.
  • static: The variable persists in memory across multiple function calls.
  • extern: Tells the compiler that a global variable is defined in a different file.
  • register: A hint to the compiler to store the variable directly in the CPU register for maximum speed (rarely used in modern C).

title: C Enums Revisited
content:
Enums (Enumerations)

Readable Constants

Enums allow you to define named integer constants. They make your code significantly easier to read and maintain by replacing confusing magic numbers with human-readable words.

Enum Days
#include <stdio.h>

enum Days {Mon = 1, Tue, Wed, Thu, Fri};

int main() {
  enum Days today = Wed;
  
  // Wed automatically becomes 3 because Mon was set to 1
  printf("Today is day number: %d\n", today);
  return 0;
}

title: C Bitwise Operations
content:
Bitwise Operators

Level 0 Logic

Bitwise operators manipulate data directly at the raw binary bit level. For example, a left shift (<<) shifts all bits to the left, which is a lightning-fast way of multiplying a number by 2.



Bit Shifting
#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
  int x = 5;      // Binary: 0000 0101
  int y = x << 1; // Binary: 0000 1010 (Which is 10 in decimal!)
  
  printf("5 shifted left by 1 is: %d\n", y);
  return 0;
}

title: C Memory Mgmt
content:
Memory Management Revisited

Safety First

Dynamic memory functions (malloc(), calloc(), realloc(), and free()) are essential. However, you must always check if the memory allocation actually succeeded before trying to use it. If the computer is out of RAM, malloc will return a NULL pointer.

Safe Allocation
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

int main() {
  int *x = malloc(5 * sizeof(int));
  
  // ALWAYS check for NULL!
  if (x == NULL) {
    printf("Memory allocation failed!\n");
    return 1; // Exit program with an error code
  }
  
  printf("Memory allocated successfully.\n");
  free(x);
  return 0;
}

title: C Dynamic Arrays
content:
Accessing Dynamic Memory

Flexible Data Structures

After allocating memory with malloc() or calloc(), you can treat the pointer exactly like an array. This allows you to build highly flexible data structures whose sizes can be determined while the program is actually running, rather than hard-coded before compilation.

Dynamic Array Indexing
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

int main() {
  // Allocate an array of 4 integers
  int *arr = calloc(4, sizeof(int));
  
  // Use standard array brackets [] to access the memory
  arr[0] = 100;
  arr[1] = 200;
  
  printf("Index 0: %d\n", arr[0]);
  printf("Index 1: %d\n", arr[1]);
  
  free(arr);
  return 0;
}

title: C Complete Summary
content:
C Final Summary — Additional Concepts 🚀

Course Completed! 🏆

You have fully conquered the C programming language! This final section covered the deepest mechanics of the language, expanding your ability to write hyper-efficient, real-world C applications.

  • Compiler Deep Dive: Mastered the Preprocessor, Macros, and Header files.
  • Memory Control: Leveraged Storage Classes (auto, static, extern) to manage variable lifespans.
  • Advanced Pointers: Used Pointers to seamlessly traverse Arrays and manage dynamic memory safely.
  • Low-Level Optimization: Utilized Bitwise operators for maximum CPU performance.

With HTML, CSS, JavaScript, SQL, Python, Java, PHP, and now C under your belt, your platform is officially a massive, fully-featured Computer Science bootcamp!

title: C Pointers & Arrays
content:
C Pointers & Arrays Deep Dive

The Memory Connection

The name of an array acts exactly like a pointer to its first element. Because of this, you can combine pointers and arrays to efficiently access and update array data without relying on traditional index brackets.



Array Iteration via Pointer
#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
  int myNumbers[4] = {25, 50, 75, 100};
  int *p = myNumbers;
  
  for (int i = 0; i < 4; i++) {
    // *(p + i) gets the value at the exact memory address
    printf("Index %d: %d\n", i, *(p + i));
  }
  return 0;
}

title: C Double Pointers
content:
Pointer to Pointer

Multiple Indirection

A pointer to a pointer (double pointer) stores the memory address of another pointer rather than a standard variable. This adds another level of indirection, which is heavily used in building dynamic 2D arrays and linked lists.



The ** Operator
#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
  int n = 10;
  int *p = &n;      // p holds address of n
  int **pp = &p;    // pp holds address of p
  
  // Dereference twice to reach 'n'
  printf("The original value is: %d\n", **pp);
  return 0;
}

title: C Function Pointers
content:
Function Pointers

Passing Behavior

Functions reside in memory just like variables do! Function pointers store the address of a function. This allows for incredibly flexible program flow, such as passing a function as an argument to another function (known as a Callback).



Calling a Function via Pointer
#include <stdio.h>

// A standard function
int add(int a, int b) { 
  return a + b; 
}

int main() {
  // Declare a function pointer 'fp' that takes two ints and returns an int
  int (*fp)(int, int);
  
  // Point 'fp' to the 'add' function
  fp = add;
  
  // Execute the function using the pointer
  printf("Result: %d\n", fp(5, 3));
  return 0;
}

title: C Dynamic Memory
content:
Dynamic Memory Allocation

Controlling the Heap

Dynamic memory functions allow allocation during run time using malloc(), calloc(), and realloc(). Because the OS does not automatically clean this memory up, you must manually release it using free() to avoid memory leaks.



Safe Allocation
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

int main() {
  int *ptr = malloc(5 * sizeof(int));
  
  // ALWAYS check if allocation was successful
  if (!ptr) {
    printf("Memory allocation failed!\n");
    return 1;
  }
  
  printf("Memory successfully allocated.\n");
  
  free(ptr); // Release it back to the OS
  return 0;
}

title: C Accessing Memory
content:
Accessing Dynamic Memory

Pointer Indexing

Memory allocated dynamically behaves exactly like a standard array. You can use standard ptr[index] syntax or pointer arithmetic *(ptr + index) to read and write elements securely.

Using the Block
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

int main() {
  int *ptr = malloc(2 * sizeof(int));
  
  if (ptr) {
    ptr[0] = 10; // First element
    ptr[1] = 20; // Second element
    
    printf("First: %d, Second: %d\n", ptr[0], ptr[1]);
    free(ptr);
  }
  
  return 0;
}

title: C Dynamic Structs
content:
C Structs & Dynamic Memory

Heap Objects

You can allocate memory for custom structs using malloc(). This is essential when you don’t know how many objects you will need at compile time. Remember to use the arrow operator (->) to access struct members via a pointer.

Malloc for Structs
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>

struct Car { 
  char brand[50]; 
  int year; 
};

int main() {
  // Allocate exact memory for one Car struct
  struct Car *ptr = malloc(sizeof(struct Car));
  
  if (ptr) {
    strcpy(ptr->brand, "Honda");
    ptr->year = 2025;
    printf("Car: %s (%d)\n", ptr->brand, ptr->year);
    free(ptr);
  }
  return 0;
}

title: C Memory Resizing
content:
Advanced Memory Management

Reallocating Data

Real-world dynamic memory usage often involves lists that must grow or shrink over time (like appending data to a file). Using realloc(), you can expand or shrink existing data structures on the fly without losing the data already stored inside them.

title: C Compilation & Tools
content:
C Compilation & Tools

The Toolchain

C source code (.c files) is just raw text. It must be translated into machine code using a compiler like GCC or Clang. When bugs occur in compiled code, developers use heavy-duty debugging tools like gdb (GNU Debugger) to step through memory and trace execution line-by-line.

title: C Practical Tips
content:
Practical Tips for C

Avoiding Disasters

C is unforgiving. To avoid the infamous "Segmentation Fault" (crashing your program by accessing forbidden memory), follow these golden rules:

  • Check Malloc: Always check if memory allocation succeeded (if ptr != NULL) before using it.
  • Free It: Memory leaks crash servers over time. Free memory the exact moment you are done with it.
  • Pointer Math: Remember that pointer math is type-dependent. Adding 1 to an `int` pointer moves it 4 bytes, not 1 byte!
  • Use sizeof(): Never hardcode byte sizes. Always use sizeof(int) or sizeof(struct Car) to ensure cross-platform compatibility.

title: C Ultimate Summary
content:
Final C Masterclass Summary 🚀

You Did It! 🏆

Congratulations! You have completed the ultimate C programming masterclass. You have tackled the most difficult concepts in computer science and now truly understand how hardware and software communicate.

  • Core: Syntax, Operators, Control Flow, and File I/O.
  • Memory: Pointers, Double Pointers, Function Pointers, and Arrays.
  • The Heap: Advanced Dynamic Memory Management (malloc, calloc, realloc).
  • Architecture: Structs, Macros, Bitwise Logic, and the Compilation Pipeline.

You now have a rock-solid foundation in systems programming. Every other language you learn from this point forward will feel incredibly easy!
